message.content: {"top_matches":[{"player":"peacock-pink-777","probability":0.6},{"player":"badger-red-827","probability":0.3},{"player":"otter-blue-1497","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["I would only accept trades that increase my own payout and decline those that hurt me — peacock and badger both mostly follow this rule (peacock declines many unfavorable offers; badger accepts beneficial offers).","As a proposer I’d generally propose offers that are profitable for myself; peacock’s proposals are consistently self-favoring even when others decline.","I tend to be cautious and selective rather than impulsive; otter sometimes accepts deals that reduce their payout, which is less like how I would behave."],"uncertainty_notes":["Small number of rounds — limited data to fully characterize strategies.","Players may make occasional mistakes or experiment, so single counterintuitive actions (e.g., otter accepting a losing trade, badger proposing a losing offer) could be noise.","Private valuations and random selection among multiple acceptors mean observed responses conflate strategy with risk preferences and luck."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"owl-yellow-7660","probability":0.60},{"player":"whale-blue-4588","probability":0.35},{"player":"iguana-red-1137","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["Proposes trades that are (given their private values) clearly net-beneficial for the proposer (consistent with payoff-maximizing behavior).","Accepts trades only when they increase the player’s own payout; declines when trades are neutral or harmful.","Refuses or declines proposals that would make them worse off (or only marginally better), indicating careful evaluation of own private values before accepting."],"uncertainty_notes":["Limited number of rounds and some proposals were declined by others, so observed behavior is noisy and may reflect others’ strategies rather than pure preferences.","Iguana displayed some inconsistent/irrational moves (accepted a clearly harmful trade and made a counterintuitive offer), which reduces my confidence that they match my own rational behavior.","Some decisions (especially declines of near-indifferent trades) could reflect risk preferences, mistakes, or coordination attempts rather than strict payoff maximization."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"buffalo-orange-6610","probability":0.8},{"player":"monkey-pink-6874","probability":0.15},{"player":"goose-purple-9985","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["Buffalo consistently proposed and accepted trades that increase their private payoff (offering GREEN to buy RED when RED is more valuable to them), and declined proposals that would have cost them — consistent with value-maximizing, rational play.","Monkey often proposed sensible trades (offering GREEN to buy RED) but also accepted at least two deals that reduced their own payout, suggesting either occasional mistakes, risk-taking, or attempts to signal/cooperate; partly rational but inconsistent.","Goose’s behavior is the most inconsistent with rational payoff-maximization (accepted at least one clearly losing trade and declined others unpredictably), suggesting noisy or non-standard play."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only a small number of turns observed; behavior could be noisy or driven by out-of-game motives (experimentation, confusion, spite, or reciprocity).","Random selection when multiple acceptances can affect incentives and observed outcomes, making inferred preferences noisier.","Some proposals (e.g., monkey’s round 0) deviate from value-maximizing moves and could reflect typos/mistakes rather than stable strategy; this reduces confidence in exact type labels."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"ram-red-5745","probability":0.7},{"player":"dolphin-purple-6605","probability":0.2},{"player":"iguana-orange-9224","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Proposes deals that clearly increase own payout and accepts only mutually beneficial offers (ram’s proposals were profitable and were not accepting bad offers).","Generally declines proposals that are not in own interest (ram and dolphin mostly declined unfavorable offers).","Avoids self-harming trades (I would not have made iguana’s round 2 proposal that reduced payout)."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only three rounds observed — limited data to fully characterize strategy or learning over time.","Some players (dolphin) accept surprisingly neutral trades; could reflect risk tolerance or incomplete attention, which is hard to distinguish from deliberate strategy.","Iguana’s mix of sensible and self-harming moves could reflect experimentation or mistakes; this increases uncertainty about whether apparent patterns are stable."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"goose-purple-9985","probability":0.65},{"player":"buffalo-orange-6610","probability":0.25},{"player":"monkey-pink-6874","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["Mostly accepted only trades that increased her own payout and proposed trades that convert GREEN to RED (consistent with a high private value for RED).","Buffalo made mutually beneficial proposals but showed inconsistent acceptance behavior (sometimes accepting indifferent trades, sometimes declining similar offers).","Monkey consistently accepted trades that let him offload low-value RED for GREEN and made aggressive proposer offers that were frequently rejected (consistent with a low private value for RED)."],"uncertainty_notes":["We only observe a small number of rounds, so one odd proposal (goose proposing a loss in Round 2) could be noise, mistake, or strategic testing. ","Private motives and risk preferences are unobserved; responses may reflect randomization or experimentation rather than strict payoff maximization."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"camel-pink-767","probability":0.7},{"player":"alligator-orange-9612","probability":0.2},{"player":"butterfly-yellow-2139","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Proposes trades that are clearly net-positive for themselves while also being acceptable to at least one likely partner (e.g., camel’s successful 2 GREEN for 3 RED).","Regularly accepts proposals that increase personal payout and rejects ones that would reduce it — a straightforward payoff-maximizing strategy.","More proactive than alligator (who is cautious and only accepts when clearly beneficial) and less prone than butterfly to make extreme, one-sided offers that others will likely decline."],"uncertainty_notes":["I only observe a few rounds and cannot see players’ decision processes or beliefs about others’ private values.","Responses could reflect risk preferences or beliefs about random selection when multiple accept — not just pure payoff maximization.","Some decline/accept choices might be driven by signaling or experiment-specific heuristics rather than strict expected-value calculations, so match is probabilistic."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"bat-purple-4553","probability":0.7},{"player":"tiger-yellow-3019","probability":0.25},{"player":"unicorn-pink-1559","probability":0.05}],"behavioral_basis":["bat-purple-4553 consistently accepted trades that increased their own payoff (trading GREEN for RED when RED value is high), and proposed trades that were at least slightly profitable — behavior consistent with payoff-maximizing, opportunistic play I would use.","tiger-yellow-3019 repeatedly proposed to buy RED by offering GREEN and declined others' offers, showing a consistent, conservative strategy to acquire higher-value RED chips; this is somewhat similar to my preference for consistent, value-driven proposals when opportunities to improve payoff appear scarce.","unicorn-pink-1559 made proposals and acceptances that were often neutral or slightly costly to themselves (accepting or proposing trades that reduced their payout), which looks more generous or noisy than the economically rational behavior I would typically follow."],"uncertainty_notes":["Relatively small number of observed rounds/proposals limits confidence in inferred motives.","Some acceptances may reflect mistakes or randomness (ties broken randomly), not stable preferences.","Private chip values are known here but players may have had other considerations (risk preferences, exploration, misunderstandings) that are unobserved."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"iguana-blue-1059","probability":0.55},{"player":"squid-purple-9957","probability":0.30},{"player":"owl-green-168","probability":0.15}],"behavioral_basis":["Proposes trades that clearly increase own payout by converting low-value chips into higher-value chips (e.g. offering RED to buy GREEN) — consistent, payoff-maximizing proposal strategy like iguana and squid, with iguana’s repeated similar offers matching my expected play.","Tends to accept only trades that increase my private payout (owl accepted only when the trade gave them higher-value RED for GREEN) — observed accept/decline patterns match a value-driven decision rule I would use.","Persistent proposer behavior: making repeated offers of the same profitable type (iguana did this twice) matches my tendency to repeat strategies that successfully get accepted.","Makes some exploratory or suboptimal proposals occasionally (one declined iguana proposal and one declined squid proposal), consistent with trying borderline offers to see responses."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only three rounds per proposer observed — small sample to fully characterize strategic style.","Responses reflect private values we see here, but actual in-game noise or other-regarding preferences (fairness, spite) could alter behavior in ways not observable.","Random selection when multiple accept means acceptance behavior could mask strategic signaling; some accepted trades may reflect opportunism by partners rather than pure preference alignment."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"parrot-pink-5400","probability":0.6},{"player":"dog-green-8079","probability":0.3},{"player":"turtle-red-9966","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["parrot-pink-5400 consistently proposes trades that clearly increase their own payout (e.g., giving low-value chips to get higher-value chips) and typically declines offers that would lose value — matches a straightforward self-interested strategy I would use.","dog-green-8079 also behaves strategically: repeatedly proposes favorable trades for themselves (offering low-value BLUE to buy higher-value RED) and accepts profitable or neutral offers from others — similar to my tendency to both propose and accept only advantageous deals.","turtle-red-9966 often accepts deals that reduce their payout and makes several proposals that others decline, suggesting either different preferences, mistakes, or risk-seeking behavior — less like the cautious, value-maximizing approach I would take."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed behavior spans only three rounds; some accepts/declines could be mistakes or misunderstandings rather than deliberate strategy.","Random selection when multiple acceptors exist and hidden private information can make individual motives ambiguous.","My judgment assumes a value-maximizing, risk-neutral playstyle; if I were risk-seeking or had different hidden utilities my match could differ."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"unicorn-blue-7096","probability":0.6},{"player":"peacock-purple-5231","probability":0.3},{"player":"badger-green-4722","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["unicorn consistently proposes and accepts trades that increase their private expected value (gives low-value RED to get high-value BLUE; declines deals that would lose value) — matches a straightforward EV-maximizer.","peacock often accepts trades that convert their low-value BLUE into higher-value RED or GREEN and proposes profitable trades, showing opportunistic/value-maximizing behavior though sometimes declining when inventory or timing issues may matter.","badger often proposes sensible trades (buying BLUE by offering GREEN) but accepted at least one trade that reduced their payout, suggesting noisier or less strictly value-maximizing behavior."],"uncertainty_notes":["We only observe a few rounds; behavior could be stochastic or dependent on private inventory levels not fully reconstructible from public payouts.","Tie-breaking and random partner selection can make some accepted responses look irrational from payout changes alone.","Players may have social preferences (reciprocity, spite) or risk/timing considerations that mimic or deviate from pure EV-maximization, increasing uncertainty about true strategy."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"peacock-red-3668","probability":0.6},{"player":"otter-blue-7516","probability":0.3},{"player":"cat-orange-2462","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Frequently proposes to acquire RED (highest private value for peacock) by offering lower-valued chips (GREEN/BLUE) — a clear profit-seeking strategy I would use if I valued RED most.","Accepts trades that clearly increase personal payout and declines ones that are unfavorable — matches the pattern of selective acceptance shown by peacock and otter.","Makes repeated similar proposals (buying RED with GREEN) after success, and scales proposal size moderately — reflects a straightforward, payoff-driven bargaining style I favor."],"uncertainty_notes":["I only observe actions, not players' private reasoning or risk preferences, so inferred motives may be approximate.","Small number of rounds and some surprising proposals (e.g., cat offering GREEN to buy BLUE) leave ambiguity about opportunistic vs. noisy behavior.","Random selection when multiple accept means some accepted outcomes may reflect luck rather than deliberate partner choice."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"duck-red-8937","probability":0.6},{"player":"whale-pink-487","probability":0.3},{"player":"iguana-red-1430","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Duck (duck-red-8937) consistently behaved like a selfish, payoff‑maximizer: proposed trades that would yield large positive gains for themselves (e.g., offering low‑value PURPLE to buy many GREEN) and accepted respondent offers that clearly increased their payout (e.g., accepting Blue8 for Red8). This matches a straightforward rational profit‑maximizing strategy I would use.","Whale (whale-pink-487) also proposed profitable deals for themselves (multiple proposals that net positive to the proposer) and executed some beneficial trades, so they are my second‑best match — generally self-interested but with a few puzzling declines.","Iguana (iguana-red-1430) sometimes made or accepted trades that reduced their own payout (e.g., proposing BLUE2 to buy RED3 which decreased their payout when executed), suggesting occasional suboptimal or experimental moves; that behavior is least like my typical rational play."],"uncertainty_notes":["Small number of rounds and some proposals were randomly resolved when multiple players accepted, so observed selections may hide intended strategic differences.","Players might be experimenting, making mistakes, or using other-regarding preferences not visible from payouts alone.","I only observe outcomes and public chip holdings; private reasoning or risk preferences cannot be directly observed, so assignment is probabilistic."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"ram-purple-8899","probability":0.60},{"player":"owl-yellow-9160","probability":0.30},{"player":"lion-yellow-9203","probability":0.10}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently proposes mutually beneficial trades that increase own payout while often attracting at least one partner (proactive, market-making behavior).","Regularly accepts trades that clearly increase personal payout and declines those that are harmful — straightforward value-maximizing behavior with occasional unsuccessful proposals.","Owl is opportunistic in proposing high-gain offers for itself and accepts positive or neutral deals; similar to my style but slightly less consistently strategic than Ram.","Lion appears value-seeking but is more constrained by inventory timing (and had at least one decline despite the trade being beneficial earlier), making it a less close match."],"uncertainty_notes":["I infer motives from observed proposals and accept/decline patterns but cannot see players' private strategic considerations (e.g., risk preferences or planned future trades).","Some declines were driven by inventory constraints (no chips left to trade) rather than preference; that complicates mapping behavior to stable strategy.","Random selection among multiple acceptors and the small number of rounds mean some actions (accepting/declining) could reflect noise rather than stable preference."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"buffalo-yellow-4552","probability":0.55},{"player":"goose-green-9189","probability":0.35},{"player":"bear-red-8738","probability":0.1}],"behavioral_basis":["Prefer proposing trades that increase my own payout (offering low-value chips to obtain higher-value chips).","Willing to accept offers that clearly increase my private payout.","Tend to propose opportunistic trades even if acceptance is uncertain (but prefer targeting trades that are plausibly attractive to at least one other player)."],"uncertainty_notes":["Only three rounds observed — limited data on strategic adaptation over time.","Cannot see players' private reasoning or risk preferences, only revealed accept/decline and payout changes.","Some proposals that look suboptimal for responders may have been targeted at specific players; interpreting intent is uncertain."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"bear-blue-8468","probability":0.6},{"player":"otter-yellow-9944","probability":0.4}],"behavioral_basis":["Consistently proposes trades that increase proposer's own payout (offers lower‑value chips for higher‑value ones) — a clear profit-seeking pattern.","Accepts trades that are net-positive for the responder and declines when accepting would reduce own payout or when lacking the required chips (inventory constraints explain some declines).","Avoids obviously self-harming proposals; does not exhibit the surprising mistake-like proposer move shown by dog-red-9959 in the final round."],"uncertainty_notes":["I only observe actions, not internal reasoning or risk preferences — similar rational patterns by bear and otter are hard to perfectly distinguish from this small sample.","Inventory constraints (chips left after earlier trades) cause some declines that look non‑strategic unless tracked — these limits reduce clarity about intent.","One player (dog-red-9959) makes an anomalous self‑harmful proposal, which could be error, experimentation, or different objectives; such possibilities increase uncertainty about which behavior I most closely match."]}

{"top_matches":[{"player":"badger-blue-8654","probability":0.5},{"player":"cat-pink-5829","probability":0.3},{"player":"butterfly-yellow-2785","probability":0.2}],"behavioral_basis":["I behave as a myopic value-maximizer who proposes exchanges that increase my own payout and accepts only trades that raise my expected payout.","badger-blue-8654 consistently proposed trades that converted lower-value chips into his highest-value chips (BLUE/RED) and accepted trades that clearly increased his payout, matching a straightforward value-maximizing approach.","cat-pink-5829 similarly proposed and accepted deals that improved her payout (selling low-value RED for higher-value BLUE/GREEN); her pattern of accepting advantageous offers fits my typical decision rule but with slightly more selective acceptance than badger.","butterfly-yellow-2785 mostly behaved like a value-maximizer (accepting and proposing beneficial swaps) but declined at least one trade that would have increased her payout noticeably, suggesting either non-myopic/strategic concerns or noisy play, which makes her less like my default behavior."],"uncertainty_notes":["Observed data is limited to only three rounds and some acceptances involved randomness when multiple players accepted (so true preferences behind acceptances could be partially obscured).","Players may have non-myopic motives (punishing others, signalling) or make mistakes; I assumed primarily payoff-maximizing, which may not capture such motives.","Tie-breaking (indifferent trades) and stochastic selection among multiple accepters introduce ambiguity about whether declines were deliberate rejections or marginal/indifferent choices."]}
